section.title: Asa's Later Years
section.sectionRationale: The same Asa who once cried out to the LORD against Zerah's million man army now, decades later, faces a lesser threat from Baasha of Israel and instead pays Ben-hadad of Aram to break his treaty and attack Israel from the north. The seer Hanani rebukes him for relying on a foreign king rather than the LORD, and Asa, enraged, imprisons the seer and oppresses some of the people. Even his final illness becomes another test: diseased in his feet, Asa seeks only physicians and not the LORD, and dies in the forty-first year of his reign.
section.thesis: Asa's tragic decline shows that a heart once fully devoted can still drift into relying on human alliances rather than God, and that resisting correction only compounds the failure, a sober warning that yesterday's faith in crisis does not automatically secure today's faithfulness.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Asa's alliance with Ben-hadad against Baasha, verses 1 to 6; Hanani's rebuke and Asa's rage, verses 7 to 10; and Asa's illness, death, and burial, verses 11 to 14.

units.label: Asa's alliance with Ben-hadad against Baasha
units.note: silver and gold from the treasuries

verses.text: In the thirty-sixth year of Asa's reign, Baasha king of Israel went up against Judah and fortified Ramah to prevent anyone from leaving or entering the territory of Asa king of Judah.
So Asa withdrew the silver and gold from the treasuries of the house of the LORD and the royal palace, and he sent it with this message to Ben-hadad king of Aram, who was ruling in Damascus:

"Let there be a {{beritCovenant|treaty}} between me and you as there was between my father and your father. See, I have sent you silver and gold. Now go and break your treaty with Baasha king of Israel, so that he will withdraw from me."
And Ben-hadad listened to King Asa and sent the commanders of his armies against the cities of Israel, conquering Ijon, Dan, Abel-maim, and all the store cities of Naphtali.
When Baasha learned of this, he stopped fortifying Ramah and abandoned his work.
Then King Asa brought all the men of Judah, and they carried away the stones of Ramah and the timbers Baasha had used for building. And with these materials he built up Geba and Mizpah.

At that time Hanani the seer came to King Asa of Judah and told him, "Because you have relied on the king of Aram and not on the LORD your God, the army of the king of Aram has escaped from your hand.
Were not the Cushites and Libyans a vast army with many chariots and horsemen? Yet because you {{samak|relied}} on the LORD, He delivered them into your hand.

For the {{ayin|eyes}} of the LORD roam to and fro over all the earth, to show Himself strong on behalf of those whose hearts are fully devoted to Him. You have acted foolishly in this matter. From now on, therefore, you will be at war."
Asa was angry with the seer and became so enraged over this matter that he put the man in prison. And at the same time Asa oppressed some of the people.

units.label: Asa's illness, death, and burial
units.note: he sought the physicians, not the LORD

verses.text: Now the acts of Asa, from beginning to end, are indeed written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel.

In the thirty-ninth year of his reign, Asa became diseased in his feet, and his disease became increasingly severe. Yet even in his illness he did not seek the LORD, but only the {{rapha|physicians}}.
So in the forty-first year of his reign, Asa died and rested with his fathers.

And he was buried in the tomb that he had cut out for himself in the City of David. They laid him on a bier that was full of spices and various blended perfumes; then they made a great fire in his honor.

beritCovenant.gloss: Covenant, treaty. Asa appeals to a berit that had existed between his father and Ben-hadad's father, a political treaty he now revives with a foreign king rather than renewing his own earlier covenant to seek the LORD, chapter 15, a telling substitution of one kind of covenant for another.

samak.gloss: To rely, lean upon. Hanani's rebuke pointedly recalls that Asa once samak, relied, on the LORD against the vast Cushite army, chapter 14, making his present reliance on Ben-hadad instead of God an especially foolish and inexcusable contrast with his own earlier faith.

ayin.gloss: Eye, eyes. Hanani declares that the ayin of the LORD roam over all the earth to show Himself strong for those whose hearts are fully devoted, a vivid image of God's constant, searching attentiveness, making Asa's failure to rely on Him not a matter of God's absence but of Asa's own misplaced trust.

rapha.gloss: To heal; here used of physicians, those who practice healing. The text's pointed contrast, he did not seek the LORD but only the rapha, physicians, does not condemn medical care itself but names the deeper failure, that even in mortal illness Asa's instinct was to seek human help alone rather than first seeking God.

basic.context: Decades after relying wholly on the LORD against an overwhelming Cushite army, Asa faces a much smaller threat from Baasha of Israel and responds very differently, stripping the temple and palace treasuries to bribe Ben-hadad of Aram into breaking his own treaty and attacking Israel from the north. It works militarily, but the seer Hanani confronts Asa, reminding him that relying on the LORD once brought total victory over impossible odds, so relying on Aram now was foolish, especially since the LORD's eyes search the whole earth to strengthen the fully devoted. Rather than repenting, Asa imprisons Hanani and oppresses some of the people. Two years later, diseased in his feet, Asa again seeks help everywhere except from the LORD, turning only to physicians, and dies soon after.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a specific political and personal decline late in an otherwise faithful king's reign; it is not a blanket condemnation of medical treatment or foreign diplomacy as such. What is theologically significant is the contrast the Chronicler draws deliberately between the younger Asa who relied on God against impossible odds and the older Asa who relied on human alliances against a lesser threat and then hardened his heart against correction, showing that past faithfulness does not guarantee present faithfulness.
basic.originalLanguages: Asa revives a political berit, treaty, with Aram rather than his own earlier covenant to seek the LORD, prompting Hanani's rebuke that he once samak, relied, on God but now does not. Hanani insists the LORD's ayin, eyes, roam the whole earth seeking whom to strengthen, making Asa's choice inexcusable, and the narrative's final indictment is that in his illness Asa sought only the rapha, physicians, never turning back to the LORD.
basic.typology: Asa's earlier reliance on God against Zerah, followed by his later reliance on Ben-hadad against Baasha, previews the church's ongoing temptation to trust worldly alliances rather than Christ, and Hanani's imprisonment for speaking truth anticipates the rejection faithful prophetic voices, and ultimately Christ Himself, would repeatedly suffer at the hands of those they confronted. Asa's refusal to seek the LORD even in mortal illness contrasts with Christ, the true Physician who is Himself sought first and who heals both body and soul.
basic.christ: Hanani's declaration that the LORD's eyes roam the earth to strengthen the fully devoted anticipates Christ's own promise that the Father sees in secret and rewards genuine faith, and stands as a rebuke to every substitute reliance on worldly power rather than on God. Asa's tragic refusal to seek the LORD in illness contrasts sharply with Christ, the true and greater Physician who welcomes the sick who come to Him first, rather than last, for healing of both body and soul, Mark 2:17.
basic.god: God remains faithful and ready to show Himself strong for those whose hearts are fully devoted to Him, His eyes searching all the earth for such people. Yet He does not force reliance on Himself, and He allows the consequences of misplaced trust, war rather than the peace faithful reliance had once secured, to unfold as both discipline and warning, even for a king who had once known His deliverance firsthand.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 15:16-24 gives the parallel account of Asa's conflict with Baasha and alliance with Ben-hadad. 2 Chronicles 14:9-13 records Asa's earlier, contrasting reliance on the LORD against Zerah. 2 Chronicles 16:9's declaration that God's eyes roam the earth echoes Zechariah 4:10. And Mark 2:17 shows Christ as the true physician who calls sinners, not the healthy, to seek Him.
basic.oneStory: The same Asa who once cried out to the LORD against impossible odds later relies on a foreign king against a lesser threat, and when confronted, hardens his heart rather than repenting, even seeking only physicians rather than God in his final illness. This sober decline warns every believer that past faithfulness must be renewed daily, and it points forward to Christ, the true Physician and object of unwavering trust, whom Asa's story urges every reader to seek first, in crisis and in health alike.

context.literary: The chapter is built around a stark contrast with chapter 14, deliberately recalling Asa's earlier reliance on the LORD, verse 8, to expose the folly of his later reliance on Aram, verses 1 to 6, before tracing the downward spiral from rebuke, verses 7 to 10, to final unrepentant illness and death, verses 11 to 14, a shape that turns Asa's whole later reign into a cautionary tale framed by his own earlier faith.
context.historical: Ramah lay just a few miles north of Jerusalem, and Baasha's fortification there threatened to cut off Judah's access to trade routes and pilgrims heading to Jerusalem, explaining the severity of Asa's response. The thirty-sixth year note in verse 1 is chronologically difficult, since Baasha, according to 1 Kings 16:8, died around Asa's twenty-sixth year, leading many scholars to understand the years in this chapter as reckoned from the division of the kingdom rather than from Asa's own accession.
context.canonical: This chapter parallels 1 Kings 15:16-24 but the Chronicler adds Hanani's prophetic rebuke and its aftermath, verses 7 to 10, material entirely unique to Chronicles, reflecting his consistent pattern of supplying prophetic commentary that explains why events unfolded as they did, and of contrasting a king's earlier faithfulness with later compromise to teach his readers the ongoing necessity of seeking the LORD.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn the Chronicler's readers, through the sobering example of a genuinely devoted king's later decline, that reliance on the LORD must be renewed continually and cannot rest on past experience alone, and that resisting correction when confronted only compounds failure rather than resolving it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical political and personal failure; it does not prescribe that all foreign alliances or all medical treatment are sinful, since seeking physicians is not itself condemned elsewhere in Scripture. What is prescriptive and enduring is the call to rely on the LORD first and foremost in every circumstance, whether small or great, and to receive correction humbly rather than reacting in anger.

debates.question: Does verse 12's statement that Asa sought only the physicians and not the LORD condemn medical treatment itself?

views.label: Condemns misplaced exclusive trust, not medicine itself
views.case: The problem the text identifies is not seeking physicians but seeking them instead of the LORD, an exclusive reliance that excluded God entirely; elsewhere Scripture assumes the legitimacy of medical care, such as Luke the physician's presence with Paul, so the critique targets Asa's heart posture, not the medical profession.

views.label: A specific critique reflecting the ancient association of certain healing practices with pagan religion
views.case: Some interpreters note that physicians in the ancient Near East sometimes practiced medicine intertwined with pagan ritual or divination, so Asa's turn to physicians rather than the LORD could reflect a turn toward such compromised practices specifically, not medicine in a modern, secular sense.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the fundamental problem is exclusive reliance that shut out the LORD entirely, not the use of medical help as such; the passage warns against any substitute, whether political, medical, or otherwise, that displaces reliance on God rather than accompanying it.

study.originalLanguages: Asa revives a political berit, treaty, with Aram rather than renewing his own earlier covenant to seek the LORD, prompting Hanani's pointed reminder that Asa once samak, relied, on God against overwhelming odds. Hanani insists the LORD's ayin, eyes, search the whole earth to strengthen the fully devoted, making Asa's later choice inexcusable, and the chapter's final indictment is that in illness Asa sought only the rapha, physicians, never turning back to the LORD who had once delivered him so completely.

typology.type: Asa's earlier total reliance on God against Zerah, later abandoned for reliance on Ben-hadad
typology.fulfillment: A warning against trusting worldly alliances rather than Christ, the only sure refuge, echoed throughout the New Testament's calls to steadfast faith.

typology.type: Hanani imprisoned for speaking a hard, true word
typology.fulfillment: The rejection faithful prophetic voices, and ultimately Christ Himself, suffered for confronting sin with truth.

study.christ: Hanani's declaration that the LORD's eyes roam the earth to show Himself strong for the fully devoted anticipates Christ's own teaching that the Father who sees in secret rewards genuine faith, and it rebukes every substitute reliance on worldly power in place of trust in God. Asa's tragic refusal to seek the LORD even in his final illness, turning only to physicians, stands in sharp contrast to Christ, the true and greater Physician who came precisely to be sought by the sick and needy, declaring that it is not the healthy who need a physician but the sick, Mark 2:17.
study.god: God remains ready and eager to show Himself strong on behalf of those whose hearts are fully devoted to Him, His eyes searching the whole earth for such people. Yet He does not compel reliance on Himself, and He permits the natural consequences of misplaced trust, ongoing war rather than the peace earlier faithful reliance had secured, as both discipline and a sobering lesson, even for a king who had once experienced His deliverance directly.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of Asa's conflict with Baasha and his alliance with Ben-hadad.
Records Asa's earlier, contrasting reliance on the LORD against the Cushite invasion.
Echoes the image of the LORD's eyes ranging throughout the whole earth.
Christ, the true physician, calls sinners, not the healthy, to come to Him, the pattern Asa failed to follow in his final illness.

study.oneStory: The same Asa who once relied wholly on the LORD against an overwhelming army later trusts a foreign king against a lesser threat, and when confronted, hardens his heart rather than repenting, even seeking only physicians instead of God in his final illness. This sober decline warns that yesterday's faith must be renewed today, and it points every reader toward Christ, the true Physician and only sure object of trust, in crisis and in health alike.

sermon.title: Asa's Folly
sermon.why: Preached August 18, 1991 on 2 Chronicles 16, this message from Desiring God works directly through Asa's later reliance on Ben-hadad instead of the LORD, drawing out exactly the contrast this study highlights between Asa's earlier faith and his later folly.

quiz.q: How did Asa respond to Baasha's fortification of Ramah?

options.text: He took silver and gold from the temple and palace treasuries and paid Ben-hadad of Aram to break his treaty with Baasha and attack Israel instead.
options.note: Verses 2-4 describe this exact response.

options.text: He cried out to the LORD in prayer as he had done against Zerah.
options.note: This is precisely what Hanani rebukes Asa for failing to do this time, in contrast to his earlier response to Zerah.

options.text: He immediately surrendered to Baasha without resistance.
options.note: Asa took decisive action, but through a foreign alliance rather than surrender or reliance on the LORD.

options.text: The chapter records no response from Asa.
options.note: Verses 2-6 detail Asa's response in full.

quiz.q: What was Hanani's central criticism of Asa's actions?

options.text: That Asa relied on the king of Aram rather than on the LORD, unlike his earlier reliance on God against the vast Cushite army.
options.note: Verses 7-8 make this exact comparison and criticism.

options.text: That Asa had not built enough fortified cities.
options.note: Hanani's criticism concerns misplaced reliance, not insufficient fortification, which Asa had already done extensively.

options.text: That Asa had failed to remove any idols from Judah.
options.note: Asa had removed many idols earlier, chapters 14-15; Hanani's rebuke concerns this specific incident of relying on Aram.

options.text: The text records no criticism of Asa from Hanani.
options.note: Verses 7-9 record an extended, specific rebuke.

quiz.q: How did Asa respond to Hanani's rebuke?

options.text: He became enraged, imprisoned the seer, and oppressed some of the people at the same time.
options.note: Verse 10 records this exact, troubling response.

options.text: He humbled himself immediately, as he had done after Shemaiah's earlier rebuke of Rehoboam's Judah.
options.note: The opposite occurred; Asa reacted with anger and imprisonment rather than humility.

options.text: He rewarded Hanani for his honesty.
options.note: Asa imprisoned Hanani rather than rewarding him, a stark contrast to a humble response.

options.text: The chapter records no reaction from Asa at all.
options.note: Verse 10 explicitly describes his angry reaction.

quiz.q: What happened during Asa's final illness?

options.text: He became severely diseased in his feet, and even then he sought only physicians, not the LORD.
options.note: Verse 12 records this exact detail.

options.text: He immediately turned to the LORD in prayer for healing.
options.note: The text explicitly states the opposite; he sought only physicians, not the LORD.

options.text: He recovered fully and reigned many more years.
options.note: The text states he died in the forty-first year of his reign, shortly after the onset of this illness, not that he recovered and reigned on.

options.text: The chapter gives no details about Asa's final illness.
options.note: Verses 12-13 give specific detail about his illness and death.

quiz.q: What does Hanani say about the LORD's eyes, and what does it mean?

options.text: That the eyes of the LORD roam over all the earth to show Himself strong on behalf of those whose hearts are fully devoted to Him.
options.note: Verse 9 gives this exact statement, emphasizing that God actively seeks out and strengthens devoted hearts.

options.text: That God is unaware of events happening on earth.
options.note: The statement affirms the opposite, God's constant, active attentiveness across the whole earth.

options.text: That God only watches Israel and ignores the rest of the world.
options.note: The text specifically says His eyes roam over all the earth, not Israel alone.

options.text: The chapter contains no statement about God's attentiveness.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly makes this statement.

questions: Asa once relied wholly on God against overwhelming odds but later trusted a foreign alliance against a lesser threat. Where might you be tempted to trust in your own past faithfulness rather than actively relying on God today?
Hanani's rebuke was true but hard to hear, and Asa responded with anger rather than humility. How do you typically respond when someone lovingly confronts you with an uncomfortable truth?
Hanani declared that God's eyes roam the earth to strengthen the fully devoted. What would it look like for your heart to be more fully devoted to God this week, in ways only He would see?
Even in his final illness, Asa sought physicians but not the LORD. Where do you turn first when you are in crisis, and is God included in that first response?

This story is a sober warning that past faithfulness does not guarantee present faithfulness. What practices help you keep seeking God freshly and consistently, rather than resting on how you have trusted Him before?

translationNotes.note: The thirty-sixth year of Asa's reign creates a chronological difficulty, since Baasha's death is dated earlier in 1 Kings 16:8; many scholars understand years in this section as reckoned from the division of the kingdom under Rehoboam rather than from Asa's own accession.
Forms of the Hebrew word berit, covenant, appear twice in this verse, translated here as treaty, referring to the political agreement Asa invokes with Ben-hadad rather than a covenant with the LORD.
The text's pointed contrast, he did not seek the LORD but only the physicians, critiques Asa's exclusive reliance on human help rather than condemning medical treatment as inherently wrong.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 16:1-14. No em dashes or en dashes remain; all such marks in the source were converted to commas.
